Fnrn Por-rrrcAt- Pnecrrcps CoMMrssroN
-128 J Street r Sr.rire 620 o Sacrarnento. CA 95g14-2-129
(916) 322--5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
April 5,2010
Jason Kaune
Nielsen Merksamer Parrinello
Mureller & Naylor, LLP
23-50 Kerner Blvd, Suite 250
San Rafael,CA94gol
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-10-052
Dear Mr. Kaune:
This letter responds to your request for advice on blnalf of the Sarr Joaquin Comnrorr
Sertse Coalition (a recently formed project of Citizens for Reliable and Affbrda-ble Electricity. a
Califomia public benefit nonprofit corporation, and Pacific Gas and Electric Company)
regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act-").1-
Please be aware that the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct.
(Regulation 18329(bX8XA).) Therefore, nothing in this letter shoulcl be con.struecl to evaluare
any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions provided apply only to
prospective actions.
Moreover, the Commission will only provide formal written advice to a person lvhose
duties under the Act are in question, or to that person's authorized representative. The
Ctlmmission does not provide advice to a third party about a public official's duties un6er the
Act. (Section 83114; regulation 18329.) Thus, we cannot respond to your specific questions
conceming the duties of the South San Joaquin Irrigation District.
However, we can provide informal assistance with respect to yollr client's prospective
obligations under the Act. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity
provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 831 14; reguiation 18329(cX3).)
' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 1000 rhrough g l014. All statutory
ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicatecl. 'fhe regulations of the Fair potitical practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8ll0 through lli997 of Title 2 of the Calitbrnia Cocle of Regulations. All
regulabry ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Cotle of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
Re
;td


File No. I-10-052
Page2
QUESTTONS
(1)(a) Should payments for communications to the public that urge local citizens to
oppose the "change of services" application filed with the San Joaquin IApCO by the South San
Joaquin [rrigation District be disclosed under the Act even if the proposal is not initiated by a
petition?
(1Xb) Should payments for billboards, websites, mailings, radio, and newspaper
advertisements, movie theater advertisements, phone calls, and other communications to the
public that urge local citizens to oppose a "change of services" application filed with the San
Joaquin LAFCO by the South San Joaquin Irrigation Disrrict be disclosed?
(2) When do such disclosure obligations begin? Is the disclosure triggered when
expenses have been incurred or when communications are made to oppose the proposal?
(3) Should the disclosures be filed monthly, semi annually, or quarterly?
(4) What forms and types of statements should be filed?
(5) Should amounts disclosed include the payments to public relations firms and
campaign consultants, and compensation to employees when engaging in direct contacts and
grassroots lobbying when made to influence the action of LAFCo?
FACTS
The South San Joaquin Irrigation District (the "district") is a special district and
governmental agency that provides irrigation and domestic water services to South San Joaquin
County. On September 3, 2009, the district's Board of Directors approved a Resolution of
Application to submit an application to San Joaquin LAFCO. Undir this application, the district
would expand its function to providing retail electricity to those living within the district,s
boundaries.2
You represent the San Joaquin Common Sense Coalition (the "coalition"), a recently
formed project of Citizens for Reliable and Affordable Electricity, a California public benefrt
nonprofit corporation, and Pacific Gas and Electric Company. The coalition opposes the
application. However, while the coalition plans on opposing the application by means of
billboard advertisements, website messages, mailings, radio, and niwspaper aivertisements,
movie theater advertisements, phone banks, and other communications to the public, the
In your request for advice, you go into significant detail describing the activity of the district. As noted
above, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct or proviie third-pariy advice. (Regulation
18329.) obviously, the activity of a public agency in connection with its own application to LAFC9 piesents
significantly difTerent questions and analysis than such activity by a private corporation or group of citizens. Thus.
we do not address the obligations of the South San Joaquin Inigation District and nothing ii ttris tetter should be
construed to be commeltal ol the district's responsibility. If you believe there has been-a violation of the Act, you
may wish to file a complaint with the commission's Enforcement Division.


File No. I-10-052
Page 3
coalition has not yet commenced distributing these communications. Your letter seeks
clarification of the coalition's duties once they begin distributing the communications.
ANALYSIS
Qualification: Generally, individuals and organizations will not have campaign
reporting obligations under the Act, unless they qualify as a "committee." Section SiOtl states
that a committee is any person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly does any of
the following:
Section 82013 provides:
"Committee" means any person or combination of persons who directly or
indirectly does any of the following:
"(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand dollars ($l,OOO; or more
in a calendar year;
"(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more in a calendar year; or
"(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more
in a calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees. A person or
combination of persons that becomes a committee shall retain its status as a
committee until such time as that status is terminated pursuant to Section 84214."
The terms "contribution" and "expenditure" are defined as payments "unless it is clear
from surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." An "independent
expenditure" means an expenditure made by any person in connection with a communication
which expressly advocates the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or the
qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified measure, or taken as a whole and in
context, unambiguously urges a particular result in an election but which is not made to or at the
behest of the affected candidate or committee. (Section g2031.)
In 2008, AB 1998 (Stats. 2008, ch. 192, $ l) added Section 84251to the Act. Section
84251provides: "A payment made for 'political purposes,' as that term is used in Sections
82015 and 82025, includes a payment made for the pulpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the actions of voters or a local agency formation commission foi or againit the
qualification, adoption, or passage of a LAFCO proposal." 3 The stated purpori for the bill was
3 Prior to the 2008 addition of the specific LAFCO provisions to the Act, the rule was as follows: ..The
obligation to report expenditures in support ofor in opposition to a proposal for incorporation begins when the
proposal becomes a measure pursuant to Section 82043. A proposal for incorporation becomes a measure when the
board of supervisors orders the proposal on the ballot. (lrt re Fontana (1976) 2 FppC Ops. 25.) Atthat time,
contributions received to support or oppose the measure were reportable. Further, contributions received and
expenditures made before the proposal became a measure that were for the purpose of influencing citizens to vote
for or against the measure were recaptured in the initial campaign statement filid once the proposal became a


File No. I-10-052
Page 4
to "impose on a committee formed to support or oppose a LAFCO proposal, as defined,
requirements regarding the filing of campaign statiments. The bill would require the committee
to file monthly campaign statements from the time circulation of a petition Uegins until a
measure is placed on the ballot or the committee is terminated. After a LAFCb proposal
measure is placed on the ballot, the bill would require a committee formed to slrpport or oppose
the proposal to file those campaign statements required of other committees formea to sup^ptrt or
oppose ballot measures under the political Reform Actof 1974.
Thus, if any group of persons received $1,000 in payments for the purpose of influencing
or attempting to influence the actions of voters or a localigency formation cornmission for or
against the qualification, adoption, or passage of a LAFCO proposal, they would qualify as a
committee under Section 82013(a) and have reporting requiremints. Similarly, a person may
also qualify as a committee under 82013(b) by making $i,ooo in independent expenditures
(expenditures made in connection with a communication expressly advocating thi qualification,
adoption, or passage of a clearly identified LAFCO p.oporai, or taken as a wliole and in context,
unambiguously urges a particular result for a LAFCb pioposal) and under g2013(c) by making
$10,000 in contributions in connection with the LAFCb pioceeding. Section g4z,s1specificaiiy
states that "[a]ll requirements of this title applicable to u -"us,,.", as defined in Section gZO43,
also apply to a LAFCO proposal, as defined in Section 82o35.5,except as set forth in Section
84252;',
In2009, AB 528 (Stats. 2009, ch. 113, g 4) further expanded reporting in connection
with LAFCo proposals by conforming the application of the Act's r"po.tirg irovisions with the
definitions in the Cortese-Knox-Hertzberg Act (Government Code Sections 56000 et seq.)
governing LAFCOs. Under AB 1998, a LAFCO proposal was defined as: "[a] proposal, as
defined in Section 56069, that is inittated ot o p"iittinforfiling with a local'agencyformation
commission " (Emphasis added.) AB 528 redefined "proposal'; in Section 3ZOJS.S of the Act as
"a proposal, as defined in Section 56069, including a proceeding, as defined by Secrio iieOZlj?
These Government Code sections, which are not part ;f the Actlprovide respectively:
" 'Proceeding,' 'proceeding for a change of organi zation,' or 'proceeding
for a reorganization' means proceedings takenly the iommission for a proposed
change of org_anization or reorganization pursuant to Part 4 (commencing with
Section 57000)." (Section 56067.)
" 'Proposal' means a request or statement of intention made by petition or
by resolution of applicatton of a legtslative body or of a schooldistrici iroposing
proceedings for the change of organization or reorganization described in-the
request or statement of intention. (Section 56069, emphasis added.)
measure." ( Roberts Advice Letter, No. A-9g- 125,)
'Ag 528 also added a reference to Section 56067. However. the reference is to a more specific statute that
is "included" within the broad language ofSection 56069 and does not provide further guidance on the scope of
Section 56069.


File No. I-10-052
Page 5
Thus, the obligation to report contributions and expenditures in connection with LAFCO
proceedings apply not merely to petitions filed with LAFCO, but also any resolution of
application filed with LAFCO.
Reporting Contributions and Expenditures: Once the group qualifies as a
"committee" under section 82013(a), it will be required to file peiiodic 
"o-puign 
reporrs.
Section 84252 provides as follows:
"(a) 4 committee primarily formed to support or oppose a laico
proposal shall file all statements required under this chaptiiexcept that, in lieu of
the statements required by Sections 84200 and 84202.3, the committee shall file
monthly campaign statements from the time circulation of a petition bEgins until a
medsure is placed on the ballot or, if a measure is not placed on the bail]ot, until
the committee is terminated pllrsuant to Section 84214. The committee shall file
an original and one copy of each statement on the l5th day of each calendar
month, covering the prior calendar month, with the clerk of the county in which
the measure may be voted on. If the petition results in a measure thaiis placed on
the ballot' the committee thereafter shall file campaign statements required by this
chapter.
"(b)Jn addition to any other statements required by this chapter, a
committee that makes independent expenditures in connection with a LAFCO
proposal shall file statements pursuant to Section g4203.5.-
QUESTTONS Ai\D CONCLUSTONS
( I )(a) Should payments for communications to the public that urge lo<:al citizens to
oppose the "change of services" application be disclosed inder the Act iven if the proposal is
not initiated by a petition?
As you noted in your request letter, AB 528 amended Section 82035.5 to remove the
language limiting a "LAFCO proposal- to those proposals "initiated as a petition for filing with a
local agency formation commission." The statuti now reads (with the ."irting.ross reference to
Section 56069), that a LAFCO proposal is "a request or statement of intention made by petition
or by resolution of application of a legislative body or of a school district proposing proceedings
for the change of organization or reorganization described in the request oi stat"*".rt of
intention'" Under this broad language, a "change of services" application wotrld be considered a
LAFCO proposal.
(t)(b) Should paymentsfor billboards, websites, mailings, radio, and newspaper
advertisements, movie theater adverttsements, phone calls, and other commtmications to the
public that urge local citizens to oppose the " ihange of se:rvices" ctpplicationbe disclosed?
All expenditures of $100 or more must be disclosed on the campaign reports, including
expenditures for the purposes you describe. 
r e-- - -r


File No. I-10-052
Page 6
(2) When do such disclosure obligations begin? Is the disclosure triggered when
expenses have been tncurred or when communications are macle to oppose tii proposal?
As you noted in your letter, it is necessary to harmonize the recent amendments to
Section 82035.5 to existing Section 84252. Section 84252states that filing begins ,,from the
time circulation of apetition begins." However, the amendment to Sectioi gzo35.5 removed the
language that limited the definition of a "proposal" to those proceedings initiated as a petition
for filing in order to expand disclosure. Thus, a literal interpretation oisecti on g4151would
effectively make meaningless the amendment to Section g2035.5.
In the event of conflict between two statutes, a more recent statute will govem and take
precedence over an earlier provision. (Los Angetes Police Protective League v. City of Los
Angeles (1994) 27 Cal.App.4th 168, 178.) Thi rules of statutory construction require an
attempt to reconcile statutory provisions relating to the same subject matter whenever possible in
order to avoid conflict and give effect to every provision. (Taxpiyers to Limit Campaign
spending v. Fair pol. practices com. (1990) 3f cu.:o 744,747.).
In order to harmonize these two provisions, consistent with the rules of statutory
constntction, we consider the " time circulation of a petition begins" to alternatively mean ,.the
time that an application is filed.l' Since the application is considered a proceeding, the filing of
the application would be the trigger for aggregating contributions in orier to qualify as a
committee. Any other construction of SectionS4252would make the more recent amendments
to Section 82035.5 meaningless.
Once these contributions equal $1,000, the coalition would be a committee. Reporting
obligations attach once the coalition becomes a committee. please note, we have advised that
expenditures made prior to qualification as a committee may need to be recaptured on the first
campaign report, such as payments made to conduct u ,r*"y if the subsequ*t use of the survey
results would constitute an expenditure. (Martini Adviceletter, No. A-g3-3 7g; winkler Advice
Letter, No. 4-86-035.)
(3) should the disclosures befiled monthly, semi annually, or quarterly?
' 
Section 84252 provides that once the coalition qualifies as a committee, the coalition
must file all statements required for other committees unde, the same filing schedules. However,
in lieu of the statements required by Sections 84200 (semi-annual reports) a nd g4zoy3 (quarterly
reports), the coalition will file monthly campaign statements from the time the application was
filed until the coalition's committee is terminutid purruunt to Sectio ng42l4.
(4) What forms and types of statements should be filed?
once the coalition qualifies as a committee, the coalition must file all statements required
for other committees under the Act. However, in lieu of the statements required by Sections
84200 (semi-annual reports) and 84202.3 (quarterly reports), the coalition will file monthly


File No. I-10-052
PageT
campalgn statements from the time the application was filed until the committee is terminated
pursuant to Section 84214.
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
(5) Should amounts disclosed include the payments to public relotionsfirms and
campaign consultants, and compensation to employees when engaging in direit contacts and
grassroots lobbying when made to influence the action of l,AFCo?
Yes. Once the coalition qualifies as a committee, these expenditures would be
reportable.s (See e.g., HobbsAdvice Letter, No. A-09-1g5.)
Please note, the Commission anticipates considering clarifying regulations later this year.
If you have other questions on this matter, prease contact,* ut (glfDlzi-5660.
Sincerely,
tu
y: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
Jwwjgl
5 Please note, in the Hobbes Advice Letter, we also advised: "However, funds received and used for
payments to a LAFCO for fees and staff time do not constitute 'contributions' under Section g2015 that would be
applied to the threshold used to constitute a committee under the Act, because the funds are not used for a .political
purpose'' AswesaidinRoberts,supra,thesefundsarenotreportablebecause'thefundingofthesestudiesisnot
meant to int'luence the citizens to vote [or the LAFCoi for or against the measure. ..., the itudies simply determine
whether a proposed city is viable and considers environmental ioncerns. The committee is purchasing a product, the
studies, and is not influencing a vote or an election,' or a decision by a LAFCo member to irpprrt of oppose the
proposal."

QUESTION: (1)(a) Should payments for communications to the public that urge local citizens to
oppose the "change of services" application filed with the San Joaquin IApCO by the South San
Joaquin [rrigation District be disclosed under the Act even if the proposal is not initiated by a
petition?
(1Xb) Should payments for billboards, websites, mailings, radio, and newspaper
advertisements, movie theater advertisements, phone calls, and other communications to the
public that urge local citizens to oppose a "change of services" application filed with the San
Joaquin LAFCO by the South San Joaquin Irrigation Disrrict be disclosed?
(2) When do such disclosure obligations begin? Is the disclosure triggered when
expenses have been incurred or when communications are made to oppose the proposal?
(3) Should the disclosures be filed monthly, semi annually, or quarterly?
(4) What forms and types of statements should be filed?
(5) Should amounts disclosed include the payments to public relations firms and
campaign consultants, and compensation to employees when engaging in direct contacts and
grassroots lobbying when made to influence the action of LAFCo?

CA 95g14-2-129 (916) 322--5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886 April 5,2010 Jason Kaune Nielsen Merksamer Parrinello Mureller & Naylor, LLP 23-50 Kerner Blvd, Suite 250 San Rafael,CA94gol Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No.I-10-052 Dear Mr. Kaune: This letter responds to your request for advice on blnalf of the Sarr Joaquin Comnrorr Sertse Coalition (a recently formed project of Citizens for Reliable and Affbrda-ble Electricity. a Califomia public benefit nonprofit corporation, and Pacific Gas and Electric Company) regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act-").1- Please be aware that the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8XA).) Therefore, nothing in this letter shoulcl be con.struecl to evaluare any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions provided apply only to prospective actions. Moreover, the Commission will only provide formal written advice to a person lvhose duties under the Act are in question, or to that person's authorized representative. The Ctlmmission does not provide advice to a third party about a public official's duties un6er the Act. (Section 83114; regulation 18329.) Thus, we cannot respond to your specific questions conceming the duties of the South San Joaquin Irrigation District. However, we can provide informal assistance with respect to yollr client's prospective obligations under the Act. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 831 14; reguiation 18329(cX3).) ' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 1000 rhrough g l014. All statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicatecl. All regulabry ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Cotle of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. Re ;td File No. I-10-052 Page2 QUESTTONS (1)(a) Should payments for communications to the public that urge local citizens to oppose the "change of services" application filed with the San Joaquin IApCO by the South San Joaquin [rrigation District be disclosed under the Act even if the proposal is not initiated by a petition? (1Xb) Should payments for billboards, websites, mailings, radio, and newspaper advertisements, movie theater advertisements, phone calls, and other communications to the public that urge local citizens to oppose a "change of services" application filed with the San Joaquin LAFCO by the South San Joaquin Irrigation Disrrict be disclosed? (2) When do such disclosure obligations begin? Is the disclosure triggered when expenses have been incurred or when communications are made to oppose the proposal? (3) Should the disclosures be filed monthly, semi annually, or quarterly? (4) What forms and types of statements should be filed? (5) Should amounts disclosed include the payments to public relations firms and campaign consultants, and compensation to employees when engaging in direct contacts and grassroots lobbying when made to influence the action of LAFCo?
